title: Exodus 26
subtitle: The Four Coverings; the Frames; the Inner Veil and Outer Curtain

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — The Four Coverings of the Tabernacle

notes.note: MacArthur: The innermost curtain is made of finely twisted linen woven with blue, purple and scarlet yarn and with cherubim -- the most precious material, the royal colours, the heavenly creatures. Holiness intensifies toward the divine centre.

MacArthur: The outermost covering is the plainest and most functional -- facing the weather, not the divine presence. The tabernacle's exterior gives no indication of the glory within. The invisible interior is always the theological priority.

heb.paragraph: V.1-6: The Tabernacle curtains of fine twisted linen with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, decorated with cherubim, formed the innermost dwelling — a portable heaven. The three-colour scheme (תְּכֵלֶת tekhelet "blue/violet", אַרְגָּמָן ʾargāmān "purple", תּוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי tōlaʿat shānî "scarlet") recurs throughout the priestly materials and anticipates the colour scheme of the High Priest's garments. Together they evoke the throne room of heaven.

V.31-35: The pārōket (inner veil) separated the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place. Embroidered with cherubim, it guarded the Ark. Its tearing at Jesus's death (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45) is the moment Hebrews 10:19-20 unpacks: the veil was Christ's flesh, and its tearing means free access to the divine Presence for all. The architectural barrier of the old covenant becomes the theological open door of the new.

panels.hist: Portable tent-shrines are well attested in the ancient Near East. An Egyptian portable canopy shrine from Tutankhamun's tomb (c.1325 BC) features similar construction: wooden frames, fabric coverings, and carrying poles. The Tabernacle's portability was essential for the wilderness period — a divine dwelling that could travel with the people.
panels.ctx: The four layers of covering (fine linen with cherubim, goat hair, ram skins dyed red, durable leather) move from most glorious inside to most durable/protective outside. The invisible glory within; the weather-resistant hide without. The architecture images the theology of the hidden God: his glory is real but veiled, accessible only through the ordained approach.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The inner curtain of linen with cherubim is the decorative masterpiece of the tabernacle. Sarna notes that cherubim woven into fabric is attested in both Egyptian and Mesopotamian sacred textiles from the Bronze Age — the tabernacle's artistic vocabulary is drawn from the wider ancient Near Eastern sacred-art tradition while being theologically reoriented toward YHWH. The craftsmen work within a known aesthetic tradition; the theological interpretation is distinctively Israelite.

The goat-hair curtains (eleven, slightly larger than the ten linen curtains) form the tent proper over the linen sanctuary. Sarna notes the practical dimensions: the goat-hair covering was the standard nomadic tent material in the ancient Near East — it provides superior waterproofing and durability for desert conditions. The tabernacle's design uses the most practical available materials at each layer while reserving the finest materials for the most sacred interior.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The layered covering descriptions — from the exquisite inner linen to the protective outer hides — produce a literary effect of progressive revelation in reverse: the reader (like the worshipper) begins with the outside and works inward toward the increasingly beautiful interior. Alter notes this inside-out description of sacred space is a literary enactment of the theology: holiness increases as one approaches the divine centre.

The second covering — goat hair curtains — is slightly larger than the inner linen covering, deliberately overlapping to protect the inner curtains from weather. Alter notes the layered structure of coverings from the most precious inward to the most practical outward mirrors the tabernacle's theology: holiness intensifies toward the centre.

Calvin on the tabernacle's four coverings: 'The diversity of coverings was not without its use; for, under the veil of these coverings, God set before his people a figure of the inward and spiritual worship which he requires.' The coverings are pedagogical: the worshipper sees only the outer, ordinary material; the priest sees the inner beauty. This graduated access enacts the principle that deeper knowledge of God comes with deeper covenant access.

On the goat-hair covering: 'As the tabernacle outwardly was very mean, so God would have his people to learn to seek him under a humble and contemptible appearance.' The external modesty of the tabernacle is Calvin's anti-grandeur theological statement: God does not require opulence on the outside because his glory is internal and spiritual. The humble exterior teaches worshippers not to confuse material splendour with divine presence.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'sea cow hides' (tachash skins) discusses the identification of tachash — traditionally translated 'badger' (KJV) or 'goat' (NRSV). The LXX translates it as hyacinth-coloured hides (indicating a colour rather than a specific animal). Modern scholars suggest dugong or dolphin skins from the Red Sea region, which were used as leather in ancient Egypt and would be suitable for waterproof outer covering. The exact identification remains uncertain.

The NET note on tachash skins discusses the uncertain identification — options include dugong (sea cow), dolphin, porpoise, or a now-unknown sea creature. The note observes that whatever the animal, the outer covering's function was to provide waterproofing for the structure. The outermost layer was functional rather than ornamental.

sections.header: Verses 15–30 — The Frames and Silver Bases

notes.note: MacArthur: The acacia wood frames coated in gold provide the tabernacle's structure. The combination of natural wood and gold symbolises the union of creation and new creation that the tabernacle embodies.

MacArthur: Set up the tabernacle according to the plan shown you on the mountain. Perfect correspondence to the divine plan is the tabernacle's standard of success -- not aesthetic beauty but faithful obedience.

heb.paragraph: V.15-30: The acacia wood frames (qĕrāshîm) overlaid with gold formed the Tabernacle's walls, connected by silver sockets and gold crossbars. The combination of materials is theological: the organic wood (earth) overlaid with gold (heaven) speaks of the incarnational principle — earthly material made fit for the presence of God through covering, not replacement. The framework that holds the divine dwelling is both humble and glorious.

V.36: The outer entrance screen marks the Tabernacle as ʾōhel môʿēd — Tent of Meeting, the place of appointed encounter between God and his people. The môʿēd (appointed time/meeting) root is the same as the Festivals: both the times and the place of encounter are môʿēd. God does not meet Israel sporadically but at appointed, predictable, accessible locations — both the sanctuary and the sacred calendar are expressions of divine accessibility.

panels.ctx: The silver bases are one of the Tabernacle's deepest theological details: they were cast from the silver collected in the census ransom (Exod 30:11–16, 38:25–27). Every Israelite's ransom payment literally became part of the Tabernacle's foundation. God's dwelling rested on the redemption of his people. The connection between atonement and the dwelling is structural, not merely symbolic.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Each frame has two silver sockets (adanim). Sarna notes that the 100 silver talents used for the bases (38:27) came from the census tax — one bekah (half-shekel) per person from 603,550 registered men. Every Israelite contributed equally to the silver foundation of God's sanctuary. The equality of the contribution (same amount regardless of wealth) is itself a theological statement: all stand equally before the covenant God.

The frame construction uses connecting crossbars (beriyachim) that run the full length of the structure through rings — a structural system that distributes tension across the entire frame rather than concentrating it at individual joints. Sarna notes the engineering is sound: the tabernacle was designed to be repeatedly assembled, transported, and reassembled.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The frame construction is the tabernacle's structural engineering, described with the same precision as the ornamental furnishings. Alter notes that the repetition of dimensions and construction details throughout the tabernacle chapters serves a mnemonic function — these are practical instructions that a craftsman must be able to follow without visual aids. The elaborate verbal description substitutes for architectural drawings that do not exist.

The corner frames and crossbars create the tabernacle's structural stability. Alter notes the attention to structural engineering in the midst of theological instruction: the dwelling of the holy God must be built to last and to travel. Covenant durability requires practical construction quality.

Calvin on the wood frames: 'Let us learn that in the construction of the tabernacle, nothing was done by chance or at man's pleasure.' Every dimension, every material, every construction detail is divinely specified. Calvin reads the precise instructions as evidence that God's presence requires careful, deliberate preparation — not casual improvisation. The worship that pleases God is ordered, specified, and faithful to the divine pattern.

Calvin: The frames and crossbars are simple in design but complex in execution. The tabernacle's structure teaches that God's dwelling among his people requires both divine specification (what God commands) and human skill (what the craftsmen execute). Obedience and excellence are both required in covenant construction.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on adanim (silver sockets/bases) discusses the etymology — from adon (lord/master), the word used for 'lord' throughout the OT. The silver bases are literally 'lordship pieces' — they are the foundations on which the frame-boards stand. The note also calculates that 100 talents of silver for 100 sockets (38:27) means each socket weighed approximately 34 kg — substantial silver foundations that anchored the tabernacle structure firmly.

The NET note on the corner frames ('doubled at the bottom and joined at the top') discusses the structural interpretation. The note traces the scholarly debate about the exact construction technique and concludes that whatever the precise method, the design provided a stable, transportable rectangular structure.

sections.header: Verses 31–37 — The Inner Veil and the Outer Curtain

notes.note: MacArthur: The curtain will separate the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place. The curtain identified in Hebrews (10:20) as Christ's body -- torn to open the way into God's presence -- is this very curtain, torn from top to bottom at the crucifixion.

MacArthur: The veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place is the tabernacle's most theologically loaded object. Its function is both protective (shielding the holy from the profane) and restrictive (limiting access to once per year, one person only). Its tearing at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51) makes explicit what the architecture had always implied: access to God was always the goal.

panels.ctx: The inner veil (v.31–33) and the outer curtain (v.36–37) create two zones of the Tabernacle: the Holy Place (table, lampstand — accessed by priests daily) and the Most Holy Place (ark, mercy seat — accessed by the high priest alone, once a year). The veil is the theological centre of the structure: it marks the point beyond which ordinary human beings cannot go. The torn veil of Matt 27:51 is the Tabernacle's most dramatic moment.

cross.note: The temple veil torn from top to bottom at the crucifixion — the barrier between God and humanity removed by Christ's sacrifice.
"We have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus, by a new and living way opened for us through the curtain, that is, his body."
The author of Hebrews describes the Most Holy Place and the mercy seat explicitly, reading the entire arrangement typologically.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The parochet (inner veil) is the most theologically significant architectural feature of the tabernacle. Sarna notes it creates the Holy of Holies as the most restricted sacred space in Israelite religion — accessible only to the high priest, only on Yom Kippur, only with blood. The veil is the boundary of the unapproachable divine presence. Its tearing (Matt 27:51) signals that Christ's sacrifice has permanently removed the barrier — the once-annual access is now perpetual through the great high priest.

The veil's placement between the holy place and the most holy creates the graduated-access system that structures all subsequent Israelite worship. Sarna notes the two curtains — inner veil and outer screen — correspond to the two degrees of separation: the priests could enter the Holy Place daily, but only the high priest entered behind the veil once annually.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The chapter closes with the two curtains — the inner veil (parochet) and the outer entrance curtain (masach). Alter notes the chapter's movement from outside (coverings) to structure (frames) to interior divisions (veil, curtain) has produced a complete cross-section of the sanctuary's architecture. The reader now knows what it looks like from every angle — inside and outside, horizontal and vertical.

The two curtains — the veil separating the Holy of Holies from the Holy Place, and the screen at the entrance — create the tabernacle's architectural theology of approach. Alter notes the approach is graduated: from outside in, from common to holy, each step requiring greater priestly qualification.

Calvin on the veil: 'Under the law, access to God was prohibited; only the high priest could enter the sanctuary, and that but once a year.' The veil is Calvin's evidence that the Mosaic covenant had an inherent limitation: it restricted access rather than opening it fully. The NT's announcement that the veil is torn (Heb 10:19–20) is the definitive statement that the limitation has been removed. What was closed is now open; what was restricted is now offered freely.

Calvin on the veil: 'The veil typified the body of Christ — as Paul teaches in Hebrews 10:20. When the veil was torn at Christ's crucifixion, access to God was opened to all believers. What the veil concealed from Israel, the cross revealed to the world.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the veil as the division between the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place (Holy of Holies) discusses the Synoptic accounts of the veil's tearing (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45). The note explains the theological significance: Hebrews 6:19–20 and 9:3–8 develop the veil as the image of restricted access that Christ's high-priestly sacrifice has permanently removed. The torn veil is the enacted theology of the epistle to the Hebrews.

The NET note on the veil's placement on golden hooks on the four pillars of acacia discusses the structural detail. The note traces the veil's theological significance through the NT's interpretation: the tearing of the temple veil at Christ's death (Matt 27:51) signals the end of mediated access and the opening of direct divine access.

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: Every Israelite's ransom payment literally became part of the Tabernacle's foundation…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"The curtain will separate the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place."NIV"The curtain will separate the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place."NASB"The veil shall serve for you as a partition between the holy place and the holy of holies."

src.title: Egyptian Portable Shrines
src.quote: The shrine from Tutankhamun's tomb (c.1325 BC) has striking structural parallels: gilded wooden framework, fabric coverings, carrying poles. The Tabernacle uses the same portable-shrine technology but fills it with radically different theology.
src.note: The Tabernacle is culturally intelligible within its Egyptian context while being theologically unprecedented.

src.title: Temple Veil in Antiquity
src.quote: Josephus (Jewish War 5.5.4) describes the Temple veil as embroidered with a panorama of heaven — consistent with the cherubim-woven veil of Exod 26:31. The veil's size in Herod's Temple: 60 x 30 feet.
src.note: The tearing of this massive veil at the crucifixion was a publicly visible, catastrophic event.

rec.text: The sustained NT typological reading: the veil as Christ's body, torn to open access; the two compartments as the two covenants; the annual high priest as the type of Christ's once-for-all entry.

The English phrase "Holy of Holies" comes directly from the Hebrew superlative qōdeš haqqŏdāšîm (v.33) — the most common description of the innermost sanctuary in all subsequent Jewish and Christian theology.

rows.label: Layered Coverings: Holiness Intensifies Toward the Centre
rows.text: The tabernacle's coverings move from the most precious inward (fine linen with cherubim) to the most functional outward (hides). The architectural theology of the tabernacle: holiness intensifies toward the centre.

rows.label: The Veil: Mediated Access to the Holy
rows.text: The veil shall separate the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place. The veil's placement creates the graduated-access system that structures all subsequent Israelite worship. Its tearing at the crucifixion will be the signature of the new covenant's access.

The architectural barrier of the old covenant becomes the theological open door of the new.

The môʿēd (appointed time/meeting) root is the same as the Festivals: both the times and the place of encounter are môʿēd. debate.title: The Tabernacle and the Jerusalem Temple: Continuity or Displacement?

positions.name: Continuity: the temple fulfils the tabernacle
positions.argument: Solomon's temple is a permanent, elaborated version of the portable tabernacle. The same sacred spaces (Holy Place, Holy of Holies), the same furnishings (Ark, lampstand, incense altar, table), and the same glory-cloud (1 Kgs 8:10–11) connect the two structures. The tabernacle is not replaced but fulfilled: the mobile presence becomes the settled presence.

positions.name: Displacement: the temple represents a theological compromise
positions.proponents: Some scholars, particularly in the Deuteronomic tradition
positions.argument: The tabernacle was the legitimate worship space; the temple represents the accommodation of Israelite religion to Canaanite and ancient Near Eastern royal temple traditions. Nathan's oracle (2 Sam 7:5–7) has God questioning whether he ever asked for a house of cedar. The tabernacle's mobility was theologically appropriate to the covenant God who accompanies his people; the temple's permanence introduced a dangerous theology of guaranteed presence.
